section.title: Cast Your Burden on the Lord
section.sectionRationale: A Maskil of David marked by an especially painful betrayal, not from an enemy but from a man like myself, my companion and close friend, verses 12-14. The psalm moves from anguished longing to flee, through the shock of intimate betrayal, to the settled confidence that closes the psalm and is later quoted almost word for word in 1 Peter 5:7.
section.thesis: Betrayed by a close friend and companion, not merely an enemy, David pours out his anguish and longs to flee, yet resolves to cast his burden on the LORD, who sustains the righteous, a pattern that anticipates Jesus, betrayed by His own familiar friend.
section.frameNote: Three movements: David's anguish and longing to flee like a dove; the shock of betrayal by a close companion amid a city full of violence; and the closing resolve to cast every burden on the LORD, who will never let the righteous be shaken.

units.label: Oh, that I had wings like a dove
units.note: anguish and the longing to flee

verses.text: Listen to my prayer, O God, and do not ignore my plea.
Attend to me and answer me. I am restless in my complaint, and distraught
at the voice of the enemy, at the pressure of the wicked. For they release disaster upon me and revile me in their anger.
My heart murmurs within me, and the terrors of death assail me.
Fear and trembling grip me, and horror has overwhelmed me.
I said, “Oh, that I had wings like a {{yonah|dove}}! I would fly away and find rest.
How far away I would flee! In the wilderness I would remain. Selah
I would hurry to my shelter, far from this raging tempest.”

units.label: It is you, my companion and close friend

verses.text: O Lord, confuse and confound their speech, for I see violence and strife in the city.
Day and night they encircle the walls, while malice and trouble lie within.
Destruction is within; oppression and deceit never leave the streets.
For it is not an enemy who insults me; that I could endure. It is not a foe who rises against me; from him I could hide.
But it is you, a man like myself, my companion and close friend.
We shared sweet {{sod|fellowship}} together; we walked with the crowd into the house of God.

Let death seize them by surprise; let them go down to Sheol alive, 
for evil is with them in their homes.

units.label: Cast your burden upon the Lord

verses.text: But I call to God, and the LORD saves me.
Morning, noon, and night, I cry out in distress, and He hears my voice.

He redeems my soul in peace from the battle waged against me, 
even though many oppose me.
God will hear and humiliate them, the One enthroned for the ages, Selah because they do not change and they have no fear of God.
My companion attacks his friends; he violates his covenant.
His speech is smooth as butter, but war is in his heart. His words are softer than oil, yet they are swords unsheathed.

{{shalak|Cast}} your burden upon the LORD and He will sustain you; 
He will never let the righteous be shaken.

But You, O God, will bring them down to the Pit of destruction; men of bloodshed and deceit will not live out half their days. 
But I will trust in You.

yonah.gloss: Dove. David longs for a dove's wings to escape entirely, a natural human longing to flee overwhelming pain rather than face it, a longing Scripture takes seriously even as it leads David, by the psalm's end, to trust rather than flight.

sod.gloss: Close, intimate fellowship or shared counsel. David and his betrayer once had sod together, deep, trusted companionship, which makes the betrayal in verses 12-14 all the more devastating; this was no stranger but a confidant.

shalak.gloss: To cast, to throw. David's command to himself, cast your burden upon the LORD, pictures a deliberate, decisive action of transferring a weight he cannot carry onto the one who can, language 1 Peter 5:7 echoes almost word for word.

basic.context: This psalm captures a uniquely painful kind of suffering: betrayal not by an enemy but by a close friend, someone with whom David once shared sweet fellowship and walked into God's house side by side. David first longs simply to escape entirely, wishing for a dove's wings to fly away from it all. But the psalm does not end there; it moves through the shock of betrayal to a settled resolve to cast every burden on the LORD, who sustains the righteous.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm gives full voice to real anguish, including the natural, human longing to flee overwhelming pain, without treating that longing as sinful in itself. Its resolution comes not by escaping the situation but by transferring the weight of it to God, a pattern of prayer meant to be imitated whenever betrayal or overwhelming pressure strikes.
basic.originalLanguages: David longs for the wings of a yonah, a dove, to fly away entirely. His grief is sharpened because he once shared sod, intimate fellowship, with his betrayer. The psalm's turning point comes in the command shalak, cast, your burden upon the LORD, decisively transferring what cannot be carried alone.
basic.typology: The betrayal by a close companion who shared table fellowship, described in verses 12-14, anticipates the betrayal of Jesus by Judas, one of His own twelve disciples, a companion entrusted with intimate fellowship who nonetheless turned against Him.
basic.christ: Verses 12-14 describe betrayal by a man like myself, my companion and close friend, one who shared sweet fellowship and walked with David into God's house. This directly anticipates Judas' betrayal of Jesus, one of His own twelve disciples who shared table fellowship with Him, John 13:18, quoting the closely related Psalm 41:9. Just as David entrusted himself to God rather than taking vengeance, Jesus, betrayed by His own familiar friend, entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23, and the psalm's closing counsel, cast your burden on the LORD, is applied directly to every believer, 1 Peter 5:7.
basic.god: God is shown as the one who hears the cry of the betrayed at every hour, morning, noon, and night, and who sustains the righteous even amid ongoing treachery, never allowing them to be finally shaken, while remaining the enthroned Judge who will hold the treacherous to account.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 5:7 quotes this psalm's counsel almost word for word, casting all your anxiety on Him because He cares for you. John 13:18 shows Jesus applying kindred betrayal-by-a-friend language to Judas. Matthew 26:36-39 shows Jesus in Gethsemane entrusting Himself fully to the Father amid the anguish of imminent betrayal and death.
basic.oneStory: Betrayed by an intimate friend, David longs to flee, but instead learns to cast his burden on the LORD, who sustains the righteous. That same pattern of betrayal met by trust reaches its height in Jesus, betrayed by His own familiar friend, who entrusted Himself to the Father and now invites every burdened believer to cast their anxiety on Him.

context.literary: A Maskil, a contemplative or instructive psalm, moving from anguished lament and the longing to flee, verses 1-8, through the shock of intimate betrayal amid civic violence, verses 9-15, to settled confidence and a closing imprecation against the treacherous, verses 16-23.
context.historical: Though the heading does not name a specific event as precisely as some other psalms of David, the betrayal described, by a close companion who shared fellowship in God's house, has long been associated with the treachery of a trusted advisor, such as Ahithophel during Absalom's rebellion, 2 Samuel 15-17.
context.canonical: Its counsel, cast your burden on the LORD, becomes direct New Testament instruction in 1 Peter 5:7, and its picture of betrayal by an intimate companion anticipates the betrayal of Jesus by Judas, deepening the psalm's significance across the whole of Scripture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To express honest anguish over betrayal by an intimate friend, including the natural longing to escape entirely, while ultimately resolving that trust in God, not flight or vengeance, is the true answer to overwhelming pressure.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own particular experience of betrayal; it prescribes an enduring pattern for every believer facing overwhelming burdens, honest lament followed by deliberately casting that burden on the LORD.

debates.question: Is the wish to 'fly away' in verses 6-8 a spiritually mature response, or a temptation to avoid a difficult calling?

views.label: An honest, God-given expression of overwhelmed emotion, not itself sinful
views.case: Scripture repeatedly gives voice to the desire to escape suffering, such as Elijah's wish to die, 1 Kings 19:4, without condemning the longing itself as sin; naming the desire honestly to God is part of healthy lament.

views.label: A real temptation that the psalm itself ultimately moves past toward a better response
views.case: The psalm does not end with flight; it resolves in verses 16-23 toward calling on God and casting one's burden on Him, suggesting the wish to flee, while understandable, is not the psalm's final, commended response to betrayal.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the psalm's ultimate counsel is not escape but trust, casting the burden on the LORD who sustains the righteous, whatever place is given to the honest, initial longing to flee.

study.originalLanguages: David longs for the wings of a yonah, dove, to fly away entirely from overwhelming pressure. His anguish is sharpened because he once shared sod, intimate fellowship, with the very companion who betrayed him. The psalm turns on the command shalak, cast, your burden upon the LORD, a decisive transfer of what cannot be carried alone.

typology.type: Betrayal by an intimate companion who shared table fellowship, verses 12-14
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, betrayed by Judas, one of His own twelve disciples who shared fellowship with Him, John 13:18.

study.christ: Verses 12-14 describe betrayal by a man like myself, my companion and close friend, one who shared sweet fellowship and walked with David into God's house. This anticipates Judas' betrayal of Jesus, one of His own twelve disciples who shared table fellowship with Him, John 13:18. Just as David entrusted himself to God rather than taking vengeance, Jesus, betrayed by His own familiar friend, entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23, and the psalm's closing counsel, cast your burden on the LORD, is applied directly to every believer, 1 Peter 5:7.
study.god: God hears the cry of the betrayed at every hour, morning, noon, and night, and sustains the righteous even amid ongoing treachery, never allowing them to be finally shaken, while remaining the enthroned Judge who will hold the treacherous to account.

crossReferences.note: Casting all your anxiety on Him, because He cares for you, echoes this psalm's closing counsel almost word for word.
Jesus applies kindred betrayal-by-a-friend language to Judas at the Last Supper.
Jesus in Gethsemane entrusts Himself fully to the Father amid the anguish of imminent betrayal and death.

study.oneStory: Betrayed by an intimate friend, David longs to flee, but instead learns to cast his burden on the LORD, who sustains the righteous. That same pattern reaches its height in Jesus, betrayed by His own familiar friend, who entrusted Himself to the Father and now invites every burdened believer to cast their anxiety on Him.

quiz.q: What does David long for in verses 6-8, and why?

options.text: The wings of a dove, to fly away entirely and escape overwhelming pressure and danger.
options.note: Verses 6-8 express this vivid, honest longing to flee rather than face the crisis.

options.text: A larger army to defeat his enemies.
options.note: David's longing here is to escape, not to gain military strength.

options.text: Wealth sufficient to bribe his enemies into peace.
options.note: This is not mentioned; the longing expressed is purely to flee to a place of rest.

options.text: A quick resolution through open confrontation with his enemies.
options.note: David expresses a desire to flee and hide, not to confront his enemies directly.

quiz.q: What makes the betrayal described in verses 12-14 especially painful for David?

options.text: It came from a close companion and friend with whom he shared fellowship, not from an enemy or stranger.
options.note: David states directly that an enemy's insult he could endure, but this betrayal came from an intimate companion.

options.text: It came from a completely unknown attacker.
options.note: The text specifies the betrayer was known intimately to David, not a stranger.

options.text: It happened publicly rather than in private.
options.note: The setting is not the main point of the pain; the intimacy of the relationship is what David highlights.

options.text: It involved the loss of significant property.
options.note: The psalm's focus is relational betrayal, not material or property loss.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's counsel in verse 22, later quoted in the New Testament?

options.text: Cast your burden upon the LORD and He will sustain you; He will never let the righteous be shaken.
options.note: 1 Peter 5:7 echoes this verse almost word for word, applying it to every believer's anxieties.

options.text: Carry your burden alone, and trust no one.
options.note: This is the opposite of the psalm's actual counsel, which is to give the burden to God, not to bear it in isolation.

options.text: Avoid prayer until the crisis has fully passed.
options.note: The psalm models constant prayer, morning, noon, and night, verse 17, not avoidance of prayer.

options.text: Seek revenge personally before trusting God.
options.note: The psalm leaves vengeance to God, verse 23, rather than commending personal revenge.

quiz.q: How does the psalm's picture of betrayal by a close friend connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates Judas' betrayal of Jesus, one of His own twelve disciples who shared table fellowship with Him.
options.note: John 13:18 draws on this same theme of betrayal by an intimate companion, applying it to Judas.

options.text: It has no connection to any New Testament event.
options.note: The theme of betrayal by an intimate companion connects directly to the betrayal of Jesus by Judas.

options.text: It only applies to betrayal among modern political leaders.
options.note: The psalm's application is timeless and personal, not restricted to a particular modern political context.

options.text: It suggests that close friendship should always be avoided.
options.note: The psalm mourns a specific betrayal; it does not counsel against friendship or fellowship in general.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's ultimate resolution, despite ongoing treachery around David?

options.text: Settled trust in God, who hears, sustains, and will not let the righteous be shaken.
options.note: Verses 16-23 close the psalm in this confident trust, even while treachery continues.

options.text: Complete despair with no hope of resolution.
options.note: The psalm moves toward confident trust, not despair, by its conclusion.

options.text: A plan for David to personally punish his betrayer.
options.note: David entrusts judgment to God rather than planning personal punishment.

options.text: A resolve to permanently withdraw from all human relationships.
options.note: The psalm does not counsel total withdrawal from relationship, but trust in God amid painful ones.

questions: David honestly admits his longing to simply fly away from overwhelming pain. Is there a burden right now you long to escape entirely, and what would it look like to bring that honest longing to God rather than suppressing it?

David says an enemy's hostility he could endure, but betrayal by a close friend wounded him most deeply. Have you experienced betrayal from someone you trusted, and how does this psalm's honesty give you permission to grieve that specifically?
The psalm's turning point is a deliberate choice: cast your burden upon the LORD. What burden are you currently carrying that you need to consciously and deliberately transfer to God today?
David prays for God's justice on his betrayer rather than pursuing revenge himself. Where do you need to release a desire for personal payback and trust God's justice instead?

Jesus experienced betrayal by His own familiar friend, Judas, yet entrusted Himself fully to the Father. How does knowing Christ understands intimate betrayal firsthand change how you bring your own experience of betrayal to Him?

translationNotes.note: Sweet fellowship translates sod, a word for intimate, trusted counsel or companionship, intensifying the sense of betrayal since this was no casual acquaintance but a confidant.
Let them go down to Sheol alive echoes the judgment on Korah's rebellion in Numbers 16:30-33, invoking a similarly sudden and decisive act of divine judgment on treachery.
Cast your burden upon the LORD is quoted nearly verbatim in 1 Peter 5:7, showing the New Testament's direct dependence on this psalm's language and counsel.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 55:1-23. One verse, 19, contained two em dashes in the raw BSB text, converted to commas. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) confirmed for Psalm 55 this session; sermon status set to none.
